content.unstructuredPrompt: Write me a report on Lithuania's GDP trends.
content.structuredPrompt:
META: You are an economics analyst. Goal: a short GDP trends report.

INPUT: Use only these sources: Eurostat, Statistics Lithuania. If data is missing – say "I don't know", don't guess.

OUTPUT: 1 page, 3–5 bullet points, with links to sources. Language: English.
content.aboutText: Once the 6 blocks are familiar, RAG (answers from your documents and sources) and fact-checking let you work with real data and reduce errors. Token economy – save time and cost; spotting manipulation – ethical, reliable use. This module prepares you for one integrated project (Module 6).
content.outcomesHeading: After this module you'll be able to:
outcomes: Understand what RAG is and when to use it; specify sources in the prompt.
Know what Deep research is and how to structure multi-step queries.
Understand token usage (context window, max_tokens) and optimise prompts for length and cost.
Spot prompt manipulation and write neutral prompts.
Spot hallucinations and check AI answer accuracy (sources, cross-check, "I don't know"); link to the Quality block.
Apply it all in one project (Module 6).

slides.title: Artificial intelligence and the universe: how it all connects
slides.subtitle: AI – much more than one tool or platform.

comparisonImages.bridgeText: As Dante pictured the world in circles – so here: the deeper you go, the more powerful it gets.

left.label: Dante's universe (Divine Comedy) – 14th c.
left.explanation: Dante pictures the world as a hierarchy where each level has its own rules, limits, and purpose.
left.source: Dante Alighieri, Divina Commedia (Divine Comedy), c. 1308–1321.

right.label: AI universe
right.explanation: Artificial intelligence is a broad field. The deeper you go, the more specialised, complex, and powerful the systems become.
right.source: Various educational AI schemas (IBM, Google, NVIDIA). Summary schema – 2023–2025.

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body:
AI isn't just ChatGPT. You'll see where generative AI sits in the AI universe, and why it matters before diving into context engineering.

**Benefit:** One prompt – and AI explains the whole hierarchy with examples.

sections.heading: 🔽 Want more detail? Why start with Dante?
sections.body: It helps to see that AI has many levels – that will make it clearer later where sources and context "fit". Next – slide 3: Context engineering: how to steer AI.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Do it now (2–7 min)
sections.body:
**What to do:** Copy the prompt below, paste it into any AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) and run it.

🔘 **Copy prompt (below)**

**What you get:** An AI hierarchy (AI → ML → Neural networks → Deep learning → Generative AI) with real-life examples.

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Copyable prompt
sections.body: Paste this prompt into your AI tool and run:
sections.copyable:
META: You are an educational assistant explaining the AI hierarchy in plain language.

INPUT: Explain the AI universe – the hierarchy from broadest to narrowest. Use a metaphor (e.g. Dante's Divine Comedy universe) if it helps.

OUTPUT:
• Schema: AI → Machine learning → Neural networks → Deep learning → Generative AI
• 1–2 sentences per level + a real-life example
• Why ChatGPT is just one layer, not all of AI
• Language: [your choice]

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Check (1 min)
sections.body:
Review the AI answer and ask yourself:

• Is the **schema** clear – from broadest to narrowest?
• Does each **level** have a real-life example?
• Is it clear why **ChatGPT ≠ all of AI**?
• Could you **explain it to a colleague** in 1 minute?

**If 2 or more "no" → go back to the prompt and add to the INPUT; don't rewrite the whole prompt.**

sections.heading: 🔽 Key message
sections.body:
Both universes work the same way: **the deeper you go – the more power, but also more responsibility.**

• Not all AI is ChatGPT – that's one of the most common mix-ups
• Generative AI is just one, deepest part of the AI universe
• The metaphor helps you see "where we are" and prepares you for what comes next

sections.heading: 🔽 Terms (1): AI, machine learning, neural networks
sections.body:
**Artificial intelligence (AI)** – broad field: computer systems that do tasks that usually need human thinking. Example: an email spam filter that learns to tell spam from real mail.

**Machine learning (ML)** – part of AI that learns from data, not fixed rules. Example: Netflix recommends films based on what you watched – no one programmed each recommendation.

**Neural networks** – models inspired by the brain: many nodes (neurons) in layers. They can handle images, sound, text, etc. (multimodal). Example: your phone's face unlock – a neural network learned to recognise your face from thousands of points.

sections.heading: 🔽 Terms (2): Deep learning, generative AI
sections.body:
**Deep learning** – advanced, many-layer neural networks (10–100+ layers). They find complex patterns in large data. Example: self-driving car vision – the system sees signs, pedestrians, cars at once.

**Generative AI** – AI that **creates new content**: text, images, sound, music. This is the deepest part of the universe – where ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, DALL-E and the tools in this module live. Example: you write a prompt – AI generates an article, slides, or image.

content.footer: Next – slide 3: Context engineering: how to steer AI

slides.title: 🧩 Context engineering: how to "steer" AI
slides.subtitle: What it is, why it matters – and what OpenAI's research shows

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (15 s)
sections.body: Give AI a clear role, format, and limits – and you get better answers. Try the template below.

sections.heading: Why this?
sections.body: After the AI universe we lock in the main idea: **context = your steering wheel.** Below – how it works and why it matters. This sets you up for RAG, sources, and checking answers.

sections.heading: How do LLMs work?
sections.body: Large language models (LLMs) generate text **word by word**: input → LLM → probabilities for the next word → chosen token. Each word depends on **all previous context** – so a well-shaped prompt (role, sources, format) cuts errors and hallucination risk.
sections.imageAlt: Autoregressive language model sketch: step N (input "Rockets became", output with percentages: champions, 2024, etc., chosen "champions") and N+1 (input "Rockets became champions", chosen "2024"); example Rockets, NBA

sections.heading: What is context engineering?
sections.body:
It's a way to **give AI the information it needs** so it understands:

• what its role is,
• what it should do,
• what result you expect.

**In plain terms:** not a question to AI, but **explaining the situation** to AI.

sections.heading: What does context include?
sections.body:
• **Goal** – what we want to get
• **Role** – how AI should think
• **Limits** – what not to do
• **Extra information** – data, examples

sections.heading: 2️⃣ InstructGPT: what the research shows (illustration)
sections.body:
**What the study shows:** better context (role, format, limits) = better ratings from evaluators. Below – results from OpenAI's experiment.

Role + format + limits = roughly twice the perceived quality. Models that follow instructions better give higher-quality answers – the basis for RAG and fact-checking.

sections.heading: What was measured? Result
sections.body: OpenAI measured **output quality** (Likert 1–7 – rating scale; SFT = learning from human examples). **InstructGPT** clearly leads: smaller (1.3B) InstructGPT is rated better than larger (175B) GPT-3 without alignment – so **role, format, and limits (context engineering)** matter more than model size. **Module 4 takeaway:** when you specify role, format, and limits, AI follows instructions better – and that helps you use RAG and check answers later.

sections.heading: In one sentence
sections.body: **Good context → better AI decisions.** AI is powerful, but without context it guesses.

sections.heading: How does that help?
sections.body:
• Fewer errors and "hallucinations"
• More accurate, predictable answers
• AI becomes a work tool, not a toy
• You can use it for automation and processes, not just one-off answers

sections.heading: Try it now (1 min)
sections.body: Copy the template below into ChatGPT or Claude, fill in the bracketed parts and run – that's already minimal context. Example: [your role] = "business analyst", [one sentence] = "prepare a summary".
sections.copyable: You are [your role, e.g. business analyst]. My task today: [one sentence]. What format I want: [e.g. 5 bullet points, English].

sections.heading: Action: what to do
sections.body: **When writing prompts – specify role, format, and limits.** Try it in the next Module 4 slides or in the prompt library.

sections.heading: Sources
sections.body:
1) OpenAI (2022). Instruction following – quality ratings by model size. [openai.com/index/instruction-following](https://openai.com/index/instruction-following/).
2) Autoregressive LLM sketch: [RBC Borealis – A high-level overview of LLMs](https://rbcborealis.com/research-blogs/a-high-level-overview-of-large-language-models/).

instructGptQuality.insight: A 1.3B InstructGPT model is rated better than 175B GPT-3 by evaluators – that shows alignment strategy, not parameter count, is the main driver of perceived quality.
instructGptQuality.scaleNote: Evaluator ratings when comparing model outputs. Higher = better instruction following.

content.pipelineDiagramSubtitle:
Prompt engineering → prompt straight to model.
Context engineering → we add context and tools.

labels.availableInContextModeOnly: This step is active only in Context engineering mode.

prompt.panelDescription: Short path: goal → prompt → model → answer. Context nodes off.

context.panelDescription: Full path: prompt and context form the input; the LLM uses tools when needed.

steps.title: User's goal
steps.shortHint: Say clearly what result you need.
steps.description: Here you define what you want: ideas, plan, text, or analysis.
steps.examplePrompt: Prepare 5 marketing ideas for a logistics company in Lithuania.
steps.withoutContext: Answer is generic – it doesn't know your business situation.
steps.withContext: Answer is aimed at a clear goal and audience.

steps.title: Prompt
steps.shortHint: Instruction to the model: clear, short, unambiguous.
steps.description: The prompt says what the model should do and what format you expect.
steps.examplePrompt: Create 5 ideas. For each give a title, channel, and short explanation.
steps.withoutContext: Ideas are generic, often like common templates.
steps.withContext: Prompt uses extra information, so suggestions are more targeted.

steps.title: Context
steps.shortHint: Add facts: documents, CRM data, rules, limits.
steps.description: Context reduces guessing. The model uses what you give, not vague assumptions.
steps.examplePrompt: Context: B2B logistics, 40 staff, goal – new clients in the region, budget 1500 EUR/month.
steps.withoutContext: Model doesn't know your real situation and suggests "one size fits all" options.
steps.withContext: Model picks ideas to match your data, limits, and priorities.

steps.title: LLM
steps.shortHint: Model analyses, combines information, generates the answer.
steps.description: Processing happens here: model reads the prompt, uses context, and shapes the result.
steps.examplePrompt: Give the answer in a table: idea | why it fits logistics | first step.
steps.withoutContext: Result is smooth but less practical for real work.
steps.withContext: Result is more actionable, with clearer next steps.

steps.title: Tools / data
steps.shortHint: When you need facts, add search, files, DB, or other tools.
steps.description: Tools let the model use up-to-date or internal data that isn't in the prompt.
steps.examplePrompt: Check the last 3 months' sales CSV and suggest ideas for the TOP client segment.
steps.withoutContext: No check against real data, so the answer can be too abstract.
steps.withContext: Answer is based on real numbers and more reliable.

steps.title: Output
steps.shortHint: Final result: plan, answer, or document.
steps.description: Output shows how well the pipeline was set up: better context → more useful result.
steps.examplePrompt: At the end add 3 actions we can do this week.
steps.withoutContext: Good to start, but often needs a lot of manual tweaking.
steps.withContext: Easier to use in practice, fewer fixes.

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (1–2 min)
sections.body: In 1–2 min you'll rate your prompt on 4 criteria and get 1–2 tips. Copy the evaluator prompt below, paste your prompt, and get clear feedback.

sections.heading: Why this?
sections.body: A good prompt rests on 4 ingredients: goal, context, structure, and format. When they're clear, AI answers more accurately and practically.

sections.heading: 1. Engineering
sections.body:
One try isn't enough: set success criteria, test, and improve.

**In practice:** a few targeted tweaks beat lots of one-off prompts.

sections.heading: 2. Language
sections.body:
Clear words and concrete context reduce guessing.

**In practice:** changing the wording can change the result a lot.

sections.heading: 3. Psychology
sections.body:
Framing and audience shape how the task is understood.

**In practice:** say who the answer is for and you get more focused output.

sections.heading: 4. Communication
sections.body:
Direct instructions and clear format make the answer predictable.

**In practice:** write the steps and the format you want.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: All four work together: goal and iteration (engineering), clarity and context (language), audience (psychology), directness and format (communication). A good prompt isn't just "what I wrote" – it's whether a colleague would have understood it.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Do it now (1–2 min)
sections.body:
**What to do:** copy the evaluator prompt below and paste it into AI (ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini). Then paste your prompt and get a short rating on 4 criteria.

🔘 Copy prompt (below)

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Copyable prompt (evaluator)
sections.body: **One prompt evaluates another:** run the evaluator first, then paste your prompt. You get a 4-criteria rating and 1–2 tips.
sections.copyable:
META: You are a prompt quality evaluator. You rate on 4 criteria: goal, context/audience, structure, format.

INPUT: A single prompt will be pasted below. Reply briefly (1–2 sentences each): 1) Goal 2) Context and audience 3) Structure 4) Format.

OUTPUT: After the 4 points give 1–2 concrete tips (what to add or change). English, concise.

practicalTask.title: Steps: one prompt evaluates another
practicalTask.template:
1. Copy the evaluator prompt from the block above (3️⃣).
2. Paste it into AI (ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini).
3. Paste your prompt.
4. Get a short rating on 4 criteria and 1–2 tips.

The evaluator helps you improve your prompt.

slides.title: System prompt and Master prompt: the difference
slides.subtitle: System = AI rules; Master = your context. Together – consistent answers.

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: **Why it's worth it:** System = how AI behaves; Master = who you are. Both together = consistent answers. On this slide – **3 tasks**: load the System template, review the Master example, create your own Master. You need to try all 3.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ 3 tasks – try all of them
sections.body: **1)** Load the System prompt template (below) and set up the System in your AI tool – for your typical audience. **2)** Review the Master prompt example (about a person) – reference for the 6-block principle. **3)** Create your own Master prompt – copy the task 3 prompt, answer the questions, get your ready Master. The training works only after you've tried all 3.

sections.heading: Task 1: System prompt template (6 blocks)
sections.body: Template you can load to set up the **System** (how AI behaves). Copy below, paste into AI as System/Instructions – adapt for your typical audience.
sections.copyable:
META: You are a business assistant. You follow the rules specified below.
INPUT: You accept requests and context (data, constraints).
OUTPUT: Answer in English – bullet list or short paragraphs.
REASONING: First summarise the task, then give the solution.
QUALITY: If you don't know – say "I don't know". Don't cite unverified sources.
ADVANCED: Tone professional, concise.

sections.heading: Task 2: Master prompt example (6 blocks)
sections.body: Example **about a person** – business analyst, for a typical audience. Review the format; you can copy and adapt for yourself (change META/INPUT to match you).
sections.copyable:
META: I am a business analyst, 5 years' experience. I work with sales data and reports.
INPUT: Audience – leadership and sales team. Goals – clear reports, basis for decisions.
OUTPUT: I want answers in English, concise, with numbers or examples where needed.
REASONING: Take into account business context and readers (not technical).
QUALITY: Style professional, no unnecessary jargon.
ADVANCED: Language English.

sections.heading: Task 3: Create your own Master prompt
sections.body: Copy the prompt from the block below ("Copyable template"). Paste into AI – answer ~10 questions (who you are, what you do, for whom). AI will generate your Master Prompt text – use it in every session.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Check (1 min)
sections.body:
• Did you **load** the System template and set up the System (1)?
• Did you **review** the Master example (2)?
• Did you **create** your own Master and get the ready text (3)?
• Do you distinguish System (how AI behaves) from Master (who you are)?

If 2 or more "no" – go back to the relevant task.

sections.heading: 🔽 Want more detail?
sections.body: Two types, two purposes: **System** defines how AI behaves (rules, format); **Master** – who you are (context). Together they give more consistent and personal answers.

rows: How AI **should behave** – rules, limits, behaviour, format
**Who the user** or organisation is – context about you
Role, language rules, "don't know" rule, limits, output format
Name, profession, goals, work style, audience, communication tone
"Answer in English. If you don't know – say I don't know. Format – bullet list."
"I'm a startup founder, work with B2B clients, 10 years' experience. Audience – managers."
From session start, for all answers
In every session where it's loaded

sections.heading: Master prompt – what it includes
sections.body:
**Personal profile:** Name, profession, experience, work style, goals, audience, tone.

**Organisation profile:** Company, sector, mission, products, market, brand, communication style.

sections.heading: How to create a Master Prompt (3 steps)
sections.body: **1️⃣** AI asks you questions (who you are, what you do, for whom). **2️⃣** You answer – in text or voice. **3️⃣** AI generates the Master Prompt document – load it in every session. Popularised by: Tiago Forte, Hayden Miyamoto.

sections.heading: Before vs After
sections.body: **❌ Without Master:** repeat context every time. **✅ With Master:** AI already knows context – "Write a LinkedIn post about the product" is enough.

sections.heading: Why it's worth spending ~10 min on Master Prompt
sections.body: Time saved, more consistent results, personalised answers, more effective work from the first message.

practicalTask.templateLabel: 📋 Task 3: Create your Master Prompt – copy and run in AI
practicalTask.template:
META: You are a personal productivity consultant helping to create a Master Prompt.

INPUT: Ask me 10 questions (one at a time) so you can create my Master Prompt document. Questions should cover:
- Who I am (name, profession, experience)
- What I do (main activity, products/services)
- Who I work for (target audience)
- How I work (style, preferences)
- What I aim for (short- and long-term goals)
- How I communicate (tone, language)

OUTPUT: After my answers, generate a structured Master Prompt document that I can copy and use on any AI platform.

FORMAT: Professional, clear, English.

RULE: Ask one question at a time and wait for my answer.

slides.title: Process prompt: mapping your workflow
slides.subtitle: Define your AI workflow – steps you reuse. Optional.

content.whyBenefit: Process gives clarity to teams and decision-makers; a defined workflow is "tracks for the train" for later chains and tools.

sections.heading: 1️⃣ What's it for? What do you get?
sections.body:
**Process prompt** – a request where you ask AI to **define or create a process**: how to work in a structured way (strategy, projects, operations) or **how you use AI in your work**. When you have a defined workflow – less guessing, more **repeatable** results.

**What you get:** a skeleton of your AI workflow (3–5 steps) that you can reuse.

👉 **First step:** paste your description into the prompt below and copy – in 2–7 min you'll have your workflow.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Define your professional workflow (2–7 min)
sections.body:
👉 Describe in one or two sentences: **what you most often do with AI** at work (e.g. "I prepare weekly reports", "I write emails to managers", "I prepare 5-slide intros for clients"). Paste into the CONTEXT field below.

AI will break it into **your** step sequence – a process you can reuse.

🔘 Copy prompt (below)

**Different from "chain":** here – **your** general way of working with AI, not a single chain (tool → tool → result).

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Copyable prompt (process / workflow definition)
sections.body: Paste your description into CONTEXT below. AI will suggest **3–5 steps** – your professional workflow skeleton that you can reuse.
sections.copyable:
ROLE: You are a work process assistant.

TASK:
Help me define **my professional workflow with AI**: what steps do I do in sequence when I use AI at work? Break it into 3–5 clear steps. For each step: **purpose**, **what I input / give**, **what I get**. Result – a process I can reuse, not a one-off task.

CONTEXT:
[Paste here: what I most often do with AI at work – 1–2 sentences]

RULES:
- Steps must be **actions**, not labels
- Process must be **reusable**
- If information is missing – say so clearly

OUTPUT FORMAT:
Short numbered list: for each step – purpose, input, result.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Check (1 min)
sections.body:
• Do these steps reflect **my** real work with AI?
• Is each step an **action**, not a label?
• Can I **reuse** this process (not just for one task)?
• Would I add or change anything in my workflow?

If 2 or more "no" → go back to CONTEXT and refine the description.

sections.heading: 🔽 Want more detail?
sections.body:
• **Process prompt ≠ one chain** – here you define **your** overall process (how you work with AI), not a specific "tool A → tool B → result" chain. For chains – see slide "Structured process".
• **Process gives clarity** – teams and decision-makers understand better how AI is used.
• **Without structure AI "guesses"** – a defined workflow gives repeatable results.

sections.heading: Optional: 3 examples by area
sections.body: With a process prompt you can ask AI to create a process **for a specific area** (not "my job", but e.g. strategy, projects, operations). Below – 3 examples you can copy and adapt.

sections.heading: Example: Business strategy creation
sections.body: Create a process – AI will break it into stages (market analysis, product development, business expansion, etc.).
sections.copyable: Create a process for a mid-size technology company's growth strategy. Break it into stages such as market analysis, product development and business expansion. Highlight the most common pitfalls and how to avoid them.

sections.heading: Example: Project management
sections.body: Give a structured process – from stakeholder buy-in to implementation.
sections.copyable: Give a structured process for managing digital transformation, from initial stakeholder buy-in to implementation. Include main phases and risk management strategies.

sections.heading: Example: Operations improvement
sections.body: Give a step-by-step process – e.g. for supply chain optimisation.
sections.copyable: Give a step-by-step process for optimising supply chain operations in a retail company. Focus on cost reduction, efficiency gains and technology integration.

sections.heading: Optional: Good vs bad process prompt

rows: "Role: business strategist. Context: retail, 50 employees, UK market."
Format: table. For each stage: goal, key indicators (KPI), deadlines.

slides.title: Working with AI: structured process
slides.subtitle: One logic for all tasks: input → processing → result

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: Here – **concrete chains**: examples (tool → result) and **your chain** with instructions for steps 1, 2 and 3. One model for all tasks: **Input → Processing → Result**. You get both suggested tools and step-by-step implementation instructions.

sections.heading: Schema: from request to result.
sections.body: Take any of your tasks and break it into these 3 steps.

sections.heading: Examples from practice
sections.body: Different chains – same logic:

workflowChains.expandInput: Topic, sources or search – facts, content, CTA
workflowChains.expandOutput: Full HTML+CSS in one file, mobile, 6-block prompt

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Build your chain and get implementation instructions (2–7 min)
sections.body:
**What to do:**
👉 Decide the **result** – what you want to get and for whom (e.g. "5-slide intro for managers", "summary into email"). Write it in one sentence and paste into CONTEXT below.

AI will break it into 3 steps, suggest tools (e.g. GPT → Gamma) and **implementation instructions** – what to do in each step (what to open, what to enter, what to copy).

🔘 Copy prompt (below)

**Different from "Process prompt":** here you get **one task's** chain with implementation; "Process prompt" – for your overall work process.

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Copyable prompt (chain + implementation)
sections.body: Paste your result (one sentence) into CONTEXT below. AI will suggest a 3-step chain with tools and **implementation instructions** – what to do in each step (what to open, what to paste, what to click).
sections.copyable:
ROLE:
You are a practical chain (workflow) assistant.

TASK:
1) Turn my task into a **concrete 3-step chain** (input → processing → result). For each step **suggest a tool** (e.g. ChatGPT, Gamma, Figma, Gmail – as fits).
2) After the chain give **implementation instructions**: what to do in step 1, 2 and 3 – concretely (what to open, what to enter or paste, what to click). Briefly, step by step.

CONTEXT:
[Paste your result in one sentence: what you want to get and for whom]

RULES:
- Steps – actions, not labels
- Tools – real (ChatGPT, Claude, Gamma, Figma, Gmail, etc.)
- Implementation instructions – so I can do it in order without extra guessing

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Chain: 3 steps + for each – suggested tool. 2) Implementation: Step 1 – …; Step 2 – …; Step 3 – ….

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Check (1 min)
sections.body:
• Is the chain **concrete** (steps + tools)?
• Do I have **implementation instructions** – what to do in steps 1, 2 and 3?
• Is each step an **action**, not a label?
• Can I **assign or change** tools based on the examples above?
• Could I **reuse** this chain for another similar task?

If 2 or more "no" → go back to CONTEXT and refine the result.

sections.heading: 🔽 Want more detail?
sections.body:
• **Chain = steps + tools + implementation instructions.** In the examples above you see the result (presentation, design, video); below you get your chain and instructions for each step.
• **Start from the result** – first decide what you want to get; then AI will suggest "how" (steps, tools, implementation).
• **Process lets you control the result** – not "hope for a good answer", but get a repeatable chain with clear instructions.
• **Full AI work process (8 steps):** set goals → give context → define role → prompts → feedback → update context → create templates → end session. This slide – simplified 3-step version (input → processing → result); 8-step description – slide "Process prompt" (4.1b2).

recognitionExercise.task: Read 5 examples and for each choose **one correct layer** from the 5 terms. One best answer per example. (2–3 min)
examples: An email app automatically sorts messages into folders by predefined rules.
A bank system automatically flags suspicious transactions.
A program learns to recognise objects in photos from thousands of examples.
A voice-controlled system understands what the user said.
ChatGPT generates an email text for a client.
explanations: Works by fixed rules, doesn't learn from data – that's the broader AI field.
System learns from data (e.g. past transactions) and spots anomalies – typical machine learning.
Learning from thousands of examples and complex images – typical of deep learning (many-layer networks).
Speech and sound recognition – neural networks (often multimodal).
Creates new text – that's generative AI (not just recognition).
recognitionExercise.goal: To see that **not all AI is generative** and that ChatGPT is just **one layer** in the whole AI universe.

ctaBlock.label: Next: Method vs agent prompt – how to write so AI delivers

slides.title: Method vs agent prompt: the difference
slides.subtitle: Method = give the method; agent = AI acts as an agent with tools

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: **Method** = AI writes to your instructions (role, steps, format). **Agent** = AI acts as an agent – searches, gathers, uses tools. Below – how to tell them apart and when to use which.

sections.heading: Two approaches to the same task
sections.body: The same goal (e.g. market analysis) can be achieved with a method or an agent approach.

rows: Specifies **how** to do it – role, steps, format
Asks AI **to take action** – search, gather, analyse
Acts as agent: uses search, tools
Needed: Browse, Search, API (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini)
Gathered data, lists, facts from the web
When you have data and need analysis / structure
When you need **to find new information** in real time

sections.heading: 1️⃣ Methodological prompt – example
sections.body:
**Situation:** You have a sector and want a structured analysis.
AI generates the report from your methodology – no external search.
sections.copyable:
Role – analyst.

Carry out a deep [Sector] analysis in [country/region] context.

Provide:
1) Main market players
2) Market size and growth trends
3) Regulatory aspects
4) SWOT (strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, threats) analysis

Format: report with tables.
Language: English.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Agentic prompt – example
sections.body:
**Situation:** You need to find real data online.
AI acts as an agent: searches, gathers, returns. **Turn on search (Browse / Search) in the platform.**
sections.copyable:
Search the web and find TOP 10 [sector] companies.

For each company provide:
- Name
- CEO / head
- Annual revenue
- Contacts (email, website)

Format: table.
Sources: state where you found the information.

sections.heading: How to write an agentic prompt (full template)
sections.body: Full **agentic prompt template (5 parts)** – Role, Task in steps, Tools, Output format, Error handling – and rules and tips are in **Module 10**, slide "When to choose an agent…". There you'll find the copyable template and guidelines.

sections.heading: How to tell them apart? (check questions)
sections.body:
• **Role** – is it specified? (both can have it)
• **Result** – "give a report" (methodological) vs "find and give a list" (agentic)
• **Tools** – is search / tools needed? (agentic only)
• **Format** – methodological specifies structure; agentic specifies **what to gather**

sections.heading: In short
sections.body:
**Methodological** = AI writes from your instructions.
**Agentic** = AI acts and searches for you.

Many real tasks use **both types together** – first the agentic one gathers data, then the methodological one analyses it.

slides.title: Optional: Good and bad – instruction wording
slides.subtitle: Examples and copyable template for Custom GPT Instructions

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: Clear role, tone, and limits – good. "Help me write" with no context – bad. Here – examples and template below.

sections.heading: Good vs bad: instruction wording
sections.body:
**Good:** "You are a business writing assistant. Tone: professional. Answers up to 200 words. Language: English." – clear role, tone, limits.

**Bad:** "Help me write." – unclear who for, what context, what length. AI guesses.

**Good:** "Goal: create meeting summaries for the team. Format: bullet points, what was decided and next steps." – concrete use case and format.

**Bad:** "Be helpful." – too vague, no quality criteria.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt
sections.body: Use this template for ChatGPT or Custom GPT "Instructions" field. Fill in the [ ] fields for your goals (e.g. [business writing assistant], [up to 200 words]).
sections.copyable:
Create an instruction for a ChatGPT assistant (Custom GPT) that I can paste into the "Instructions" field.

META (role and behaviour):
- You are [ENTER ROLE, e.g.: business writing assistant, customer service expert, training content planner].
- Tone: [professional / friendly / concise – choose or describe].
- Competence: [WHAT THE ASSISTANT KNOWS – topics, domain, limits].

Goal:
- [WHAT THE ASSISTANT IS FOR: e.g. meeting summaries, answering customer queries, creating training content plans].

Context (if needed):
- Audience: [WHO IT'S FOR – e.g. team members, clients].
- Organisation / product: [BRIEF DESCRIPTION, if relevant].

Limits:
- Language: [EN / other / both].
- Answer length: [e.g. up to 200 words; or "as needed"].
- Avoid: [e.g. jargon, advertising, personal opinions].

Output format:
- [e.g. bullet points, paragraphs, table; clear actions "who / when"].

content.footer: Next – slide 15: Is your prompt good?

slides.title: Is your prompt good… or does it just look that way?
slides.subtitle: 5 principles show the real picture – you can rate it.

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: Principles are the base for the next steps. Copy the prompt below, paste yours – get a rating and 3 weak spots.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Do it now (2–7 min)
sections.body:
**What to do:**
👉 Copy the prompt below and run it in AI (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini). Paste **your** prompt into the INPUT slot – get a rating on 5 principles and a suggested improved version.

🔘 Copy prompt (below)

**Don't aim for perfect** – paste any current prompt you have.

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Copyable prompt
sections.body: Paste your prompt into INPUT – AI will rate it on 5 principles and suggest improvements.
sections.copyable:
ROLE:
You are an experienced prompt engineer and training facilitator. Your goal – critically evaluate prompt quality and suggest improvements.

TASK:
Rate the given prompt against 5 principles:
1) Clarity
2) Experimentation
3) Simple → complex
4) Context
5) Word choice

INPUT:
[prompt to evaluate – paste your prompt here]

EVALUATION FORMAT:
1. Rating table (1–5 points per principle)
2. Brief explanation of WHY that score
3. 3 main weak spots
4. Suggested improved prompt version

RULES:
- Be specific, no generic phrases
- If information is missing – state it clearly
- If the prompt is too complex – suggest a simpler first version
- Don't rewrite unnecessarily – fix only what adds real value

OUTPUT:
Structured, clear, practically usable answer.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Check (1 min)
sections.body:
• Did you get a **rating table** (1–5 per principle)?
• Are the **3 weak spots** clear and fixable?
• Is the **improved version** useful – could you use it?
• Would you **use** these principles next time before sending a prompt?

If 2 or more "no" – go back to your prompt (INPUT) or change it and run again.

sections.heading: 🔽 Want more detail?
sections.body:
**5 principles:** (1) Clarity – one goal, one task. (2) Experimentation – change one thing at a time. (3) Simple → complex – the first prompt is the base. (4) Context – who you are, who it's for, what world. (5) Word choice – verbs > abstractions ("do", not "think about").

**Why it matters:** Foundation for working with sources, agents, and deep research. Mistakes here cost more later.

**Next:** Slide 48 (RL/RLHF).

practicalTask.template:
1. Copy the evaluation prompt below ("Copyable prompt" section).
2. Open ChatGPT, Claude or Gemini.
3. Paste the evaluation prompt; paste your prompt in the INPUT place.
4. Run – you'll get an evaluation against 5 principles and 3 weak spots.
5. The evaluator and your prompt aren't rivals; use the evaluation to improve your text.

correctPromptPractice.intro: Practice: the prompt below breaks 5 principles. Spot the issues and rewrite for clarity, context, and action verbs – or press "Show solution" and compare.
correctPromptPractice.badPrompt: "Think about strategy and maybe analyse competitors and prepare a plan with all the important stuff... just do something useful."
correctPromptPractice.solutionAnalysis: 1) **Clarity** – several tasks at once, unclear goal. 2) **Simple → complex** – everything asked for straight away. 3) **Context** – no role, audience, sector. 4) **Word choice** – abstractions ("think", "do something") instead of action verbs.
correctPromptPractice.solutionCopyable: Role: market analyst, 5 years in IT sector. Do a short analysis of 3 main competitors in the Lithuanian SaaS market. Provide: (1) each competitor's strength and weakness, (2) price comparison in a table. Format: table, English, business tone.
correctPromptPractice.solutionSummary: One goal (competitor analysis), clear context (IT/SaaS, Lithuania), clear structure (table), precise verbs ("analyse", "give"). You can copy this improved version and run it.

slides.title: What is reinforcement learning (RL / RLHF)?
slides.subtitle: How AI learns from trials, errors, and human feedback

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body:
**RL (Reinforcement Learning)** – AI learns from reward.
If the result matches the goal (KPI, scores, success metrics) → it gets a "plus". If not → it adjusts behaviour.

**RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)** – AI learns from human ratings.
People compare answers and say which is better.
The model learns what counts as a good answer.

👉 GPT answer quality – result of RLHF.
👉 Your prompt triggers and steers that learned behaviour.

**Try it now**

Copy one of the prompts below (5️⃣ or 6️⃣) and run it in AI.
Watch how answer quality changes when you change the wording. #askifyouneed

sections.heading: 2️⃣ RL – reinforcement learning
sections.body:
**Without human feedback** – AI learns only from a numerical reward.

Plain explanation:
AI learns by acting in an environment and getting reward or penalty.

**Business example:** E-shop automatically adjusts prices.
**How it learns:** System raises/lowers price and checks if sales go up.
**Reward:** Number of sales (more/less).
**When to use:** When success can be measured by key metrics (KPI).

sections.heading: 3️⃣ RLHF – reinforcement learning from human feedback
sections.body:
What changes?
👉 **The human becomes the source of reward**

Plain explanation:
AI learns not only from numbers but from which answer people prefer.

How it works for GPT:
• People compare answers
• Mark the "better" ones
• Model learns behaviour rules

**Business example:** AI drafts replies to customer emails.
**How it learns:** Staff mark which reply is better for the customer.
**Reward:** Human choice ("fits / doesn't fit").
**When to use:** When quality, tone, and trust matter, not just numbers.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ RL vs RLHF – comparison
sections.body: RL = optimising numbers. RLHF = optimising human judgement.

rows: Asks: "How to look better to the user?"

sections.heading: 5️⃣ RL prompt example
sections.body:
🔘 Copy the prompt (below).

**Without human feedback.**

**Business case:** Optimise an email by clear KPIs.
**When to use:** When success can be defined by rules and numbers.
sections.copyable:
You are an AI that optimises business emails.

Goal:
Write an email that maximises open rate.

Rules:
- Up to 100 words
- Clear value proposition
- Clear CTA

Generate 3 versions and pick the best by: "clearest and shortest".

sections.heading: 6️⃣ RLHF prompt example
sections.body:
🔘 Copy the prompt (below).

**With human feedback.**

**Business case:** Choose the best email for a real customer.
**When to use:** When human opinion, tone, and trust matter.
sections.copyable:
You are an AI that creates business emails.

Task:
Generate 3 email variants for a client about AI training.

Then:
- Ask ME to choose the best variant
- Ask what I liked / didn't like

Based on my choice, generate 1 improved final email.

sections.heading: 🔽 Link to GPT, prompts, and ethics
sections.body:
Why does this matter for us?

GPT doesn't "think" – it optimises answers by:
• usefulness
• safety
• human expectations

**Prompt** = the signal the model uses to select learned behaviour.

sections.heading: 7️⃣ One-line takeaway
sections.body: GPT answers are an RLHF result – so wording, tone, and context change answer quality. If AI ignores context – add role and rules (see 5 principles).

content.footer: Next – slide 17: Parameter field

slides.title: Parameter field for prompt engineers
slides.subtitle: 6 groups: system, method, content, manipulation, quality, technical

content.introHeading: Why does the parameter field matter?
content.introBody: Prompt engineering isn't text editing – it's controlling a multi-layer system. The 6 parameter groups show where you can really have an impact – from model choice to technical settings.

blocks.name: System
blocks.desc: Where and with what you work: model, context window, tools, access.
concepts: Context window – how much information fits
blocks.tip: If the result keeps drifting – first check if the model is right and if the context window is enough.

blocks.name: Method
blocks.desc: How you generate: step-by-step, RAG, comparisons, self-check.
blocks.tip: For complex tasks specify the method: "First analyse, then compare, summarise at the end".

blocks.name: Content
blocks.desc: What you put in: context, sources, limits, examples, ethics.
blocks.tip: Bad input often leads to wrong or unreliable answers. Specify sources and limits clearly.

blocks.name: Manipulation
blocks.desc: How you influence model behaviour: role, framing, tone. Powerful but risky.
concepts: Framing – how you pose the question
blocks.tip: Use role and tone consciously. In Module 4 you'll learn to spot when wording can skew the answer.

blocks.name: Quality
blocks.desc: What result you want: detail, structure, audience, language, format.
blocks.tip: State it like a "contract" with the system: "Output: 5 bullets, English, business tone".

blocks.name: Technical
blocks.desc: Model behaviour: temperature, response length, penalties.
blocks.tip: It's not magic – it's probability control. First state the task clearly, then tune parameters.

content.practiceBody: Copy the prompt below and run it in AI. In ~2 min you'll get a parameter list in 6 groups.
content.practiceCopyable:
META: You are a prompt engineering assistant.

INPUT: List the main parameters a prompt engineer can control, grouped into 6: System, Method, Content, Manipulation, Quality, Technical. For each group give 3–5 concrete parameters or questions.

OUTPUT: English, structured (6 headings + bullets). Short, scannable.
content.tip: The prompt engineer works across the whole parameter field – from model and method to content, ethics, and technical settings. If the result is weak, the problem is almost never just "a badly written prompt" – it's a mismatch in one of the 6 groups.

slides.title: Top AI tools
slides.subtitle: Choose a tool by task type

sections.heading: Main AI tools before workflow
sections.body: Choose the tool by task type, not popularity. The right choice saves time and reduces errors.

sections.heading: Quick orientation (30 s) ⚡
sections.body:
By task, pick the right tool:

✍️ Writing / strategy → **ChatGPT**
📄 Long documents / analysis → **Claude**
📊 Word, Excel, Teams → **Copilot**
🔎 Research / images / video → **Gemini**
📑 Slides / proposals → **Gamma**

sections.heading: What questions to ask when choosing a tool?
sections.body:
**1.** What's my main task type? (Writing? Analysing? Working with documents? Preparing slides?)

**2.** Am I working with long, complex context? (If it's a 50+ page document or lots of data – you need long context.)

**3.** Am I in a specific ecosystem? (If Word, Excel, Teams – Office integration matters.)

**4.** Do I need multimodal? (Text + images + video – you need a more general model.)

**5.** Is the output a presentation or proposal? (If the end product is slides, pick a tool that generates them.)

sections.heading: How to choose?
sections.body:
**Rule:** Task → Tool → Workflow (not the other way round).

Writing / strategy → **ChatGPT**.
Data / code / long documents → **Claude**.
Office → **Copilot**.
Research / visuals → **Gemini**.
Slides / proposals → **Gamma**.

You can combine several tools in one chain. E.g.: Claude (analysis) → ChatGPT (wording) → Gamma (slides).

sections.heading: Practical recommendation
sections.body: **Pro tip:** In 80% of cases one main tool + one specialised is enough. What matters isn't having all tools – it's knowing clearly which to use at which step.

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: RAG – when AI bases the answer on **your** documents, not its own knowledge. Below – what it is and how to try it in a few minutes.

sections.heading: Agent view
sections.body: Switch between modes: Basic (input → AI → output), RAG (with database), Tools. Shows how AI uses your sources.
sections.imageAlt: AI agent system: Basic, RAG, Tools modes

sections.heading: What is RAG (3 steps)
sections.body: RAG – when AI generates the answer from **your** documents. 3 steps: search sources → select key facts → answer. Ask: "Use only the context given", "If not there – say I don't know", "Cite the source" – fewer errors.

sections.heading: Do it now
sections.body:
Take any short text (report snippet, list). Below – mini template: paste text and question, run AI.

🔘 Copy prompt (below)

Don't aim for perfect – any short text and one question.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt
sections.body: Answer only from your context. If the answer isn't there – AI will say I don't know.
sections.copyable:
Answer only based on this context. If the answer isn't in the context – say I don't know. Cite the snippet.

CONTEXT:
[paste your text here]

QUESTION:
[paste your question here]

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Does the answer rely on your context? Did AI say "I don't know" when information was missing? Did it give a reference? If any "no" – add context or run again.

sections.heading: 🔽 Optional: more on RAG and multimodal input
sections.body:
**Multimodal input and output:** Some AI accepts not only text but image, sound – useful for analysis and summaries.

**RAG in detail:** How much context fits in a query – see Token economy slide. Think: which documents repeat at work? Note one – you'll use it in Module 6.

content.footer: Next – slide 22: Open data and RAG

slides.title: Open data and RAG
slides.subtitle: Eurostat, data.europa.eu, national portals – official sources

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: RAG = query → official source → citation → conclusion. Specify sources and ask for answers with links – answers will be grounded, not "from the head".

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Do it now
sections.body: Copy the GDP example below and run it in AI (Eurostat or data.europa.eu) – you'll get an answer with dataset links. Then try other prompts from this slide.

sections.heading: One example A to Z: GDP LT vs LV
sections.body: Question: Did GDP grow faster in Lithuania than in Latvia over 2020–2024? Steps: (1) Dataset nama_10_gdp. (2) Filter: countries LT, LV. (3) Years: 2020–2024. (4) Answer with citation (dataset, indicator, link). Copy the prompt below.
sections.copyable: Use only Eurostat (ec.europa.eu/eurostat) data. Task: Did GDP grow faster in Lithuania than in Latvia over 2020–2024? Use dataset nama_10_gdp, filter countries LT and LV, years 2020–2024. Give the answer with dataset name, indicator, and link.

sections.heading: Official open data sources – 3 blocks
sections.body:
**A. EU level:** Eurostat – EU statistics (GDP, employment, trade, demography). data.europa.eu – legislation, reports, datasets. OECD – international comparative statistics.

**B. National level:** e-TAR – main source of legal acts. Parliament – official laws. Statistics office – statistics, API. Business register – legal entities, financial reports. Procurement – procurement data. Central bank – financial statistics.

**For legal acts – only the current version from e-TAR.**

**C. RAG rules:** Only official sources. Specify dataset ID or source name. Cite links. Check validity date.

sections.heading: 🔽 Other international sources
sections.body: data.gov (US) – US government data. World Bank open data – international statistics. For Baltic/EU audience Eurostat and data.europa.eu are used most.

sections.heading: Examples table
sections.body: Four typical cases: what to ask and what you get – official sources and links.

rows: "From Eurostat data: what is [country] GDP change over the last 5 years? Cite dataset or indicator."
RAG uses only official statistics; you can specify a dataset (e.g. nama_10_gdp).
"Find data.europa.eu open data on [e.g. renewable energy] in the EU. Give 3 datasets with links and short description."
Search by topic; output with links – RAG source specification.
"From official [country] registers: which are the 5 largest [sector] companies by turnover? Specify source."
RAG limited to official registers – lower hallucination risk.
"From OECD or Eurostat: compare [indicator, e.g. education spending] in 3 countries. Give figures and source links."
Several official sources in one query; links for each conclusion.

sections.heading: Mini-prompt: Eurostat template
sections.body: Use only Eurostat data. Ask for the answer with dataset names and links.
sections.copyable: Use only Eurostat (ec.europa.eu/eurostat) data. Task: [describe, e.g. GDP comparison]. Give the answer with dataset names and links.

sections.heading: Mini-prompt: open sources list
sections.body: Good option: group by category (statistics, law, business, finance, procurement), give institution, link, and whether it has API.
sections.copyable: Compile a list of official [country] open data sources suitable for RAG analysis. Group by category: statistics, law, business, finance, public procurement. For each give: institution, link, type of data, whether it has API.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Check: anti-hallucination RAG rule
sections.body:
**1.** No dataset or source link → answer is invalid.
**2.** No year or indicator code (e.g. nama_10_gdp) → answer is insufficient.
**3.** Source not official → reject.

Full topic on hallucinations – Module 7 (Hallucinations).

content.footer: Next – slide 23: Working with RAG: memory and sources

slides.title: Working with RAG: memory, external sources, and data prep
slides.subtitle: Memory, business documents, data prep – guidelines and template

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (RAG memory and sources)
sections.body: In this slide you'll get a simple rule: when AI memory is enough, when you need documents, and how to ask for answers with references, not opinions.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Memory – long-term context about your business
sections.body:
**What it is:** AI memory (Memory) – where AI stores key facts about your business: what you do, who your customers are, priorities, style.

**Example:** "My company does FMCG distribution in the Baltics. Priority is profitability and growth, not turnover."

**When to use:** When you want AI to always "know" who you are and what you do – so you don't repeat it every time.

**Important:** Memory isn't for number crunching. It's background context – no tables or documents.

sections.heading: 3️⃣ External sources – real RAG in business
sections.body:
**What it is:** Your real documents: strategy plan, financial report, sales Excel, CRM export, project list, contracts.

**Where to keep / use:** Your files, NotebookLM (Google), Trello, CRM; or NoteLM as a notes ecosystem.

**When to use:** When you want answers based on numbers and text, not generic phrases: profitability analysis, segments, risks, contracts.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Data prep – 80% of the result
sections.body:
RAG will give weak answers if documents are messy, unlabelled, and you don't ask for sources.

**Minimal business RAG standard:** clearly labelled sources (e.g. [Source 1 – 2026 Q1 Financial report], [Source 2 – Sales by region]) and a query that asks for citations.

sections.heading: 5️⃣ Do it now
sections.body:
In the prompt say: "Use both my saved context (Memory) and the documents provided." Copy the prompt below and use it with your documents.

🔘 **Copy prompt (below)**

sections.heading: 6️⃣ Copyable prompt
sections.body: Paste this prompt into AI (ChatGPT, Claude) with your documents labelled [Source 1], [Source 2]:
sections.copyable: Here is data with references: [paste text with [Source 1], [Source 2]…]. Analyse and give conclusions; for each conclusion give the source reference.

sections.heading: Decision matrix: AI memory vs documents
sections.body: In the table – typical situations: when AI memory is enough, when you need documents, and when not to rely on AI memory.

rows: You need exact facts and tables.
⚠ AI memory can be wrong or out of date.

sections.heading: 7️⃣ Check
sections.body: If AI doesn't cite sources or references are unclear – go back to data prep: add clear labels [Source 1], [Source 2] and in the prompt require "For each claim give source ID."

sections.heading: Practical workflow – typical business user
sections.body:
**1. Memory:** "My company is in construction. Priority is margin, not turnover."

**2. Documents uploaded:** Q1 financial report, Project profitability analysis.

**3. Prompt:** "Using the documents, identify the least profitable projects and suggest 3 actions to improve margin. For each claim give source ID."

sections.heading: Core logic
sections.body:
**Memory** = context about the business. **Documents** = real numbers. **Structure + citations** = decision reliability.

Without documents – it's opinion. With documents – it's analysis.

sections.heading: 🔽 Links: NotebookLM, NoteLM, Trello
sections.body: **NotebookLM** – Google AI tool for working with document sets (PDF, Docs, Slides) and RAG across sources. Link: [notebooklm.google](https://notebooklm.google/). **NoteLM** – AI notes and video tool if you prefer a notes ecosystem. Link: [notelm.app](https://www.notelm.app/). **Trello** – boards and cards; you can describe tasks as context for a RAG prompt. Link: [trello.com](https://trello.com/).

content.footer: Next – slide 24: AI tools for information search

slides.title: Optional: AI tools for information search
slides.subtitle: AI tools – from search to synthesis.

content.whyBenefit: You can do any research in 30–45 min – enter a question or upload a PDF, get answers with sources.
content.toolsIntro: Enter a question or upload a PDF – you'll get answers with sources.

tools.name: Perplexity
tools.description: Search with sources. Instead of an hour of googling – enter a question, get an answer with links.

tools.name: PaperGuide
tools.description: Talk to your PDF. Upload a report or study – get summary, conclusions, main points.

tools.name: Scite
tools.description: Does the source really support your claim? Shows how the study is cited – supports or contradicts.

tools.name: Elicit
tools.description: Finds and summarises research by topic. Enter a question – get trends, risks, examples.

tools.name: Consensus
tools.description: Searches only in research papers and answers "what does science say?" – shows if studies support or contradict a claim.

tools.name: Connected Papers
tools.description: Shows how papers connect and which "research clusters" exist; helps find key (seminal) work.

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (20 s)
sections.body: AI tools for information search and synthesis: Perplexity (search with sources), PaperGuide (PDF), Scite (citation check), Elicit (research synthesis), and two research tools – Consensus and Connected Papers. Typical run – 30–45 min.

sections.heading: Usage tips
sections.body:
**Perplexity:** Web mode – for news and business; ask for links ("Give sources for each claim"); be specific (e.g. "What 2024 trends in X market?").

**PaperGuide:** Upload PDF (report, study), give a concrete question; data extraction – for tables from PDF.

**Scite:** Enter article link or title; check "Supported" vs "Contradicted"; verify the citation matches.

**Elicit:** Start with a clear question; extract data from several PDFs into a table; ask "Give trends and risks".

**Consensus:** When you need "what does science say?" – use for health/tech/policy claims; see if research supports or contradicts.

**Connected Papers:** When you want to see the paper network and clusters – use when planning deep research or tech directions.

sections.heading: Typical run (30–45 min)
sections.body:
**4 tools = full run:**

**1.** Sources (Perplexity) – 5–10 min: enter question, gather initial sources.

**2.** PDF analysis (PaperGuide / Elicit) – 10–15 min: upload documents (reports, studies), get summaries and conclusions.

**3.** Citation check (Scite) – ~5 min: check if sources support your claims.

**4.** Synthesis (Elicit) – 10–15 min: summarise trends, risks, and examples.

**Full run on one topic** often 30–45 min.

content.footer: Next – slide 25: Data prep for RAG

slides.title: Data prep for RAG reliability
slides.subtitle: Clean-up, summaries, headings, metadata, chunks

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: 5 steps: metadata → clean-up → headings → chunks → summary. Pick a step – you'll see the prompt and copy it.

sections.heading: RAG prep pipeline
sections.body: Pick a step – you'll see the prompt and why it matters. Sequential process: metadata → clean-up → headings → chunks → summary.

sections.heading: What else do I do?
sections.body:
**1) Clean-up** – remove extra spaces, broken characters, duplicates; keep UTF-8.

**2) Summaries** – for long documents add a short summary (executive summary) at the start; RAG finds matches faster.

**3) Headings** – clear headings, section titles, lists; a short content outline – the model orients better.

**4) Metadata** – source name, date, type next to each block; RAG can cite and filter.

**5) Chunks** – split into logical chunks (paragraph, section), not too long; logical chunks = more precise search.

sections.heading: Plain-language term: chunk
sections.body: **Chunk** – one logical piece of text you split the document into for the RAG system (e.g. one paragraph, section, or topic). Instead of one long file in search – smaller, meaningful chunks; then RAG finds only the bit that matches the question. **How to use:** set the boundary by meaning (one idea/paragraph); avoid long chunks without headings; add a short heading or summary for each.

content.footer: Next – slide 26: 4 strategies

slides.title: 4 strategies that improve AI answer quality
slides.subtitle: Step-by-step, CoT, comparisons, idea tree (ToT) – with examples

sections.heading: 🎯 What's in it for me?
sections.body:
• Less chaos
• Clearer conclusions
• Structured answers instead of a stream of text
• Better RAG / Deep research quality

Works with RAG and Deep Research – it's a universal structure.

sections.heading: 1️⃣ Step-by-step
sections.body:
What I do: Ask to break the task into clear steps.

Copy and paste into AI:
sections.copyable: Give a step-by-step plan [enter your task, e.g. to launch a new marketing campaign].

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Chain of thought (CoT)
sections.body:
What I do: Ask to explain the logic, not just the result.

Copy and paste into AI:
sections.copyable: Explain the pricing strategy – from positioning to final price. Show how each step leads to the next.

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Comparisons and analogies
sections.body:
What I do: Ask to compare or explain by analogy.

Copy and paste into AI:
sections.copyable: Compare traditional and digital marketing – when is which more effective for business? Give concrete examples.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Idea tree (ToT)
sections.body:
What I do: Ask to evaluate several options and suggest the best – several branches, pick the best.

Copy and paste into AI:
sections.copyable: Evaluate 3 alternatives [for a project or investment] by risk, return, and timeframe. Suggest the best one and briefly justify.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: You're not just asking AI. You're steering its thinking structure.

content.footer: Next – slide 27: Practice: COMBO

slides.title: Practice: COMBO
slides.subtitle: How managers think is how AI should think

sections.heading: 1️⃣ Situation (typical in business)
sections.body: You have a problem: sales are flat, market is squeezing prices, the team is scattered between ideas, management needs a clear decision. AI can't give "nice text" – it has to give decision logic. That's what COMBO is for.

sections.heading: COMBO = Structured query framework
sections.body: In one prompt you combine 4 layers: **1️⃣ Role** – who is analysing? **2️⃣ Process** – how do they think? **3️⃣ Alternative evaluation** – what is compared? **4️⃣ Clear output** – what exactly do I get? This isn't a question – it's a mini strategy session in one prompt. Copy below and try.

sections.heading: Why does it work?
sections.body:
• AI can't dodge with generic phrases
• You get a decision structure, not an opinion
• The answer becomes usable in a management meeting
• You can use the same principle in the M6 project

**Bottom line:** When you use COMBO, you're not asking for advice – you're controlling the analysis architecture.

sections.heading: 🔽 Token management (want to know more?)
sections.body:
COMBO prompts can be long – respect the token limit (prompt + answer); plan max_tokens or split into several prompts. If the answer gets cut off – split into several queries with a clear sequence.

Link to Module 4 token economy (4.4) – more on tokens and context window.

practicalTask.template:
Act as a B2B business strategy consultant.

Give a step-by-step analysis of why our sales have been flat for 12 months.

Evaluate 3 possible directions:
– price cut
– new product
– new market

Compare them by:
• risk
• investment needed
• profit potential

Finally give:
– recommended direction
– 90-day action plan
– top 3 risks

content.footer: Next – slide 28: Self-check: RAG

slides.title: Self-check: RAG
slides.subtitle: Do you get it? 2–3 questions – quick check

questions.question: What is RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)?
options: A method where AI generates the answer using retrieved information from documents or databases
questions.explanation: You get RAG: the answer comes from your sources (documents, data), not from memory. If wrong – see RAG slide (4.2), "What is RAG?"

questions.question: What should you ask for in a RAG prompt when the information isn't in the sources?
questions.explanation: Correct – "I don't know" reduces hallucination risk. If wrong – see RAG slide (4.2) and the mini-template with "I don't know".

questions.question: What are the 3 main RAG steps?
questions.explanation: RAG: search sources → select chunks → generate answer. If wrong – see slide 4.2, "How it works (3 steps)".

questions.question: Why does RAG reduce hallucination risk?
options: Because AI relies on concrete documents, not just its training data
questions.explanation: RAG "anchors" the answer in sources – fewer fabrications. If wrong – see slides 4.2 and Module 7 (Hallucinations, Fact-checking).

questions.question: What does "RAG reliability depends on data quality" mean?
options: If sources are wrong or out of date, the AI answer will be too
Data quality doesn't matter in RAG
questions.explanation: Wrong or out-of-date sources = unreliable answers; so data prep matters. If wrong – see slide 4.2b.

content.footer: Next – slide 29: Deep research

slides.title: Deep research
slides.subtitle: Multi-step research, sub-questions, synthesis; Perplexity, ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini

sections.heading: 1️⃣ When do you need it?
sections.body: When the decision costs money, you need to justify to management, one answer isn't enough, or you need sources, not opinion. Deep Research ≠ a simple question to AI – it's a structured mini research (5–15 min).

sections.heading: What is it in essence?
sections.body: 4 stages in one process: (1) topic split into sub-questions, (2) source search, (3) data extraction, (4) synthesis with links. Result – analysis fit for a decision.

sections.heading: How is it different from RAG?
sections.body: **RAG:** works with your documents, answers from a given context. **Deep Research:** searches outside, compares several sources, combines into one analysis. It's not an answer – it's a report.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Deep Research prompt (copy–paste)
sections.body: Copy below and replace [TOPIC], [GOAL], [SOURCES]. Output: 1–2 page synthesis with links.
sections.copyable: Do deep research on: [TOPIC]. Goal: [e.g. justify a decision to management]. Use only reliable sources: [e.g. Eurostat, official portals, research papers]. Break the topic into sub-questions. Give a 1–2 page synthesis. For each conclusion give the source. If information is missing – say "I don't know".

sections.heading: Where does it work?
sections.body: [Perplexity](https://perplexity.ai) (Research mode), [ChatGPT](https://chat.openai.com) (Browse / Advanced Data Analysis), [Claude](https://claude.ai) (with tools), [Gemini](https://gemini.google.com) (built-in search). For academics: [Elicit](https://elicit.com), [Consensus](https://consensus.app).

sections.heading: ⚡ Quick win
sections.body: Simplest to start – copy and replace [topic]:
sections.copyable: Create a prompt for deep research on [topic].

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: Deep Research lets you not "ask AI" – but back a decision with data.

content.footer: Next – slide 30: Practical tasks: prompt sequences

slides.title: Practical tasks: prompt sequences
slides.subtitle: Sequence • Chain (CoT) • Idea tree (ToT) – 3 thinking templates

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (Prompt sequences)
sections.body: 3 ways: Sequence (step by step), Chain (CoT – logic), Idea tree (ToT – branches). Try with your [PROBLEM].

sections.heading: When do you need it?
sections.body:
When: • The task is too complex for one question • You need structure, not a spontaneous answer • You're doing Deep Research • You want to control the decision flow

Prompt sequences let you control the process, not just the result.

sections.heading: Do it now
sections.body: Copy the universal template below, replace [PROBLEM] with your task and run one of the prompts (Sequence / Chain / Idea tree). Example problems: market analysis, segmentation, strategy choice.

sections.heading: Universal template
sections.body: Copy below and replace [PROBLEM] and [sequence / chain / idea tree] as needed.
sections.copyable:
Write me a prompt [sequence / chain / idea tree] that would help solve this problem: [PROBLEM].

Make sure:
– the structure is clear
– the steps link logically
– there is a clear final decision

sections.heading: 🔽 Want more detail? (3 templates – 3 situations)
sections.body:
**1️⃣ Sequence** – Use when you need a clear action plan. Fits: market analysis, product launch, process optimisation. Logic: A → B → C → decision.

**2️⃣ Chain (CoT)** – Use when decision logic matters. Fits: pricing justification, strategy argument, explaining a decision to management. Logic: each step clearly follows from the previous.

**3️⃣ Idea tree (ToT)** – Use when you need to pick the best direction. Fits: investment decisions, evaluating several options, risk analysis. Logic: several branches → evaluation → best choice.

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: One question gives an answer. A prompt sequence gives a decision architecture.

content.footer: Next – slide 31: Combined practice: RAG + Deep research

slides.title: Combined practice: RAG + Deep research
slides.subtitle: 5–10 min – combine sources (RAG) and depth (Deep research) in two prompts.

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: **Benefit:** In 5–10 min you'll combine **RAG** (answer from sources) and **Deep research** (depth) – two prompts, one topic. Sources + depth in one mini-project.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Do it now (5–10 min)
sections.body:
**What to do:**
👉 Pick a topic that matters to you. Copy the two-prompt template below. Run **prompt 1** (RAG) – get an answer with sources. Then **prompt 2** (Deep research) – paste the first answer or say "based on the previous answer" and get an extended analysis.

🔘 Copy prompt (below)

**Don't aim for perfect** – any topic that's useful to you.

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Copyable prompt
sections.body: Two-step template – first RAG, then Deep research. Replace [topic] with yours.
sections.copyable:
PROMPT 1 (RAG):
Analyse [topic]. Use only reliable sources (Eurostat, official portals, research papers). Give your answer with links for each conclusion. If information is missing – say I don't know.

PROMPT 2 (Deep research):
Based on the previous answer, extend the analysis. What are the 3 main gaps? What do the latest (2025–2026) studies say? Provide links.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Quick check (1 min)
sections.body:
• Does the answer **include links** to sources?
• Did **prompt 2** add new insights (depth)?
• Could you **use this at work** or in study?
• Were there things the AI **couldn't** answer – and is that clear?

If 2 or more "no" – try another topic or refine the prompt (e.g. a more specific [topic]).

sections.heading: 🔽 Want more detail?
sections.body:
**3 steps:** (1) RAG – query with source requirements (Perplexity, ChatGPT Browse, etc.). (2) Deep research – follow-up question based on the first answer (sub-questions, synthesis). (3) Check – do links work, are conclusions logical?

**Self-reflection:** Did you find reliable sources? Did the follow-up question add new insights? These questions help you prepare for the Module 5 test and Module 6 project.

slides.title: Self-check: Deep research
slides.subtitle: Do you get it? 5 questions – quick check

questions.question: How does Deep research differ from a simple RAG query?
options: Deep research – multi-step process (sub-questions, search, synthesis); RAG is often one query → one answer
questions.explanation: You've got it – the difference between one query (RAG) and multi-step analysis (Deep research). If wrong – remember: Deep research = sub-questions + intermediate conclusions + final synthesis. In practice that means deeper but longer. If wrong – see slide "Deep research" (4.3), block "What is Deep research?"

questions.question: Which tools offer Deep research–style features?
questions.explanation: Good – you know the tools. Perplexity Research, ChatGPT Browse/ADA, Claude with tool use, Gemini all support multi-step research. If wrong – no problem: the main thing is knowing such tools exist and trying at least one. If wrong – see slide "Deep research" (4.3), block "Where to find it?"

questions.question: What are the main steps of Deep research?
questions.explanation: Deep research differs from simple RAG because the AI breaks the question into sub-questions, searches, draws intermediate conclusions and only then gives a final answer. If wrong – see slide "Deep research" (4.3), "Link to RAG" block.

questions.question: When is it worth using Deep research instead of simple RAG?
options: When the question is complex and you need information from several sources or perspectives
When you need a simple fact (e.g. a date or number)
When you want a faster answer
questions.explanation: Deep research fits complex, multi-step questions. For a simple fact, RAG is enough. Rule: if the question can be answered in one sentence – RAG; if you need to analyse, compare, summarise – Deep research. If wrong – see slide "Deep research" (4.3), "Task for the agent" block.

questions.question: What does "prompt sequence" mean in a Deep research context?
options: Several related prompts where each builds on the previous result (CoT, ToT)
Several unrelated prompts on different topics
questions.explanation: A prompt sequence (Chain-of-Thought, Tree-of-Thought) means each step builds on the result of the previous one. That helps the AI "think" more consistently and give a deeper answer. If wrong – see slide "Practical tasks: prompt sequences" (4.3a).
