slides.title: Token economy
slides.subtitle: Tokens, context window, max_tokens, cost – how to shorten and keep quality

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: Token = smallest unit of text the AI processes. Context window and max_tokens limit how much information "fits". 7 tips – how to shorten and keep quality. Practice below.

sections.heading: What is a token?
sections.body: A token is the smallest unit of text the AI processes. Roughly: 1 token ≈ 4 characters (English) or ~0.75 words. In other languages tokens can be shorter (e.g. with accents).

sections.heading: Concrete example
sections.body: The sentence "Artificial intelligence is changing the job market" ≈ 7 tokens (English). 1 A4 page (~2000 characters) ≈ 500–600 tokens. A whole book (~80,000 words) ≈ ~100,000 tokens.

sections.heading: Context window
sections.body: The maximum input and output text (in tokens) the model can "see" at once. When exceeded – the oldest information is lost (the model "forgets" the start of the conversation). Includes: your query + chat history + generated response.

sections.heading: 5 tips for your token budget
sections.body: 1) **Structure** – split topics into sub-topics (shorter conversations). 2) **Shorten** – precise, short prompts save tokens. 3) **Summarise** – in long chats ask for a summary. 4) **Limit output** – use max_tokens, "max 150 words", "list only". 5) **Templates** – system prompt with fixed instructions so you don't repeat.

sections.heading: Why does it matter?
sections.body: Longer context = higher cost and slower response. A short, clear prompt with clear Output limits – saves money and time. E.g. "No intro – answer only" or "Give a numbered list only (5 items)".

content.footer: Next – slide 35: Context degradation

slides.title: Context degradation: why do models "forget"?
slides.subtitle: In long conversations the AI loses focus – three mechanisms and three practices

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: **Context degradation** – why the AI "forgets" in long conversations. Three mechanisms: Lost in the Middle, attention spread, context displacement (FIFO). Three practices: checkpoint, separate chats, repeat priorities.

sections.heading: What happens under the hood: 3 context-loss mechanisms
sections.body:
**1. Lost in the Middle** – models absorb information best at the **start** or **end** of the text; information in the **middle** loses impact (research: accuracy can drop from >90% to ~40%).

**2. Attention spread** – as the number of tokens grows, the model's "attention" to each specific fact decreases.

**3. Context displacement (FIFO)** – when the technical limit is reached, the oldest information is dropped to make room for new input.

sections.heading: Business example: strategic planning
sections.body: How an important priority gets lost in a long conversation – and what risk that is for the business.

rows: **100% accuracy.** Model fully focuses on this value.
Cost-cutting options, logistics, supplier contracts discussed.
**70% accuracy.** Main priority starts to "drown" in technical detail.
Manager asks: "Which logistics plan is best?"
**Risk:** The model may suggest the cheapest option, which sharply increases workload for staff, **forgetting** the priority stated in the 1st message.

sections.heading: Research data
sections.body:
"The model's ability to identify a specific fact in long context decreases exponentially when that fact is not directly related to the latest query." — *Liu et al., Stanford University (2023)*

• **Loss of effectiveness:** In complex sessions, consistency of reasoning drops on average **~39%** over a long discussion.
• **Error accumulation:** A wrong assumption in the 5th message becomes "fact" by the 15th (hallucination chain).

sections.heading: How to reduce the risk
sections.body:
**1. Checkpoint method** – every ~10 messages ask the AI to summarise what we've agreed.

**2. Isolate context** – use separate chat windows for different projects.

**3. Repeat priorities** – restate the most important rules in each critical query (Prompt re-injection).

**Try it:** Start your next long conversation with a 1–2 sentence summary – that reduces "forgetting" risk.

sections.heading: 🔽 Want more detail? Sources
sections.body:
**Stanford / UC Berkeley (2023):** "Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts" – how models use long context; U-shaped curve (best at start and end).

**DeepMind Research:** "Long-range Language Modeling and Attention Decay" – attention decay in long contexts.

**OpenAI Technical Reports:** "GPT-4o Context Window Management and Limits" – context window management and limits.

content.footer: Next – slide 36: Self-check: Tokens

slides.title: Self-check: Tokens
slides.subtitle: Do you get it? 3+ questions – quick check

questions.question: What is the context window?
options: The maximum input and output text (in tokens) the model can see at once
questions.explanation: You've got it – the context window is how many tokens the model "sees" at once. Beyond that limit, the oldest information is lost. If wrong – see slide "Token economy" (4.4), "What is the context window?"

questions.question: How do you optimise prompts for tokens?
options: Shorten context, clear Output limits (max words), max_tokens
questions.explanation: Good – you know the main token-saving principles. If wrong – see slide "Token economy" (4.4), "7 tips" or "5 tips for token budget" block.

questions.question: What happens when prompt and answer tokens exceed the context window?
options: The oldest information is lost (the model "forgets" the start of the conversation)
The model runs slower but loses nothing
The model automatically picks a stronger model
Nothing – the context window is unlimited
questions.explanation: The context window is limited. When exceeded, the model "forgets" the oldest parts. If wrong – see slide "Token economy" (4.4), "Context window" block.

questions.question: Roughly how many characters is 1 token (English)?
questions.explanation: In English, 1 token ≈ 4 characters or ~0.75 words. That matters when planning how much text "fits" in the context window. If wrong – see slide "Token economy" (4.4), "What is a token?"

questions.question: What is the max_tokens parameter used for?
options: To limit the length of the AI response (and control cost)
questions.explanation: max_tokens limits how many tokens the AI can generate in the response. That helps control cost and keep the answer from being too long. E.g. max_tokens=500 ≈ ~375 words. If wrong – see slide "Token economy" (4.4), "Technical parameters" or "7 tips" block.

content.footer: Next – slide 37: Negative prompts (what to avoid)

slides.title: Negative prompts (what to avoid)
slides.subtitle: What to avoid and how to rewrite – vague, too short, biased prompts; examples from best practice

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: You'll learn to spot **negative prompts** (too vague, too short, no goal, biased) and rewrite them into effective ones – with a goal, context, and clear output.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Do it now (2–7 min)
sections.body:
**Task:**
👉 Take one of your old or "bad" AI queries (or make up a typical one: "help with a report", "write something") and paste it into the prompt below.

**CTA:** 🔘 Copy prompt (below)

Don't aim for perfect – use what you actually write or wrote.

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Copyable prompt
sections.body: One prompt – the AI will help you rewrite your query into a clear, controllable version (goal, context, result).
sections.copyable:
ROLE: You are a prompt-engineering assistant helping to improve queries.

TASK:
Rewrite my query below into a "good" prompt following best practices:
- Clear goal (what I want to get)
- Context (who for, where, what constraints)
- Clear output (format, length, what counts as a good result)
- No vague terms ("help", "optimise", "improve") without concrete actions

CONTEXT (my current / bad query):
[Paste your query here]

RULES:
- Keep my intent but make the query actionable and verifiable
- If information is missing – say what needs to be added
- Give 2 versions: short (1–2 sentences) and longer (with blocks)

OUTPUT FORMAT:
"Short version:" + "Longer version:" with clear blocks.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Quick check (1 min)
sections.body:
Check the rewritten prompt:

• Is it **clear** what I want (goal)?
• Is there **context** (who for, where, limits)?
• Is **every word** actionable, not abstract ("analysis", "optimise" without actions)?
• Do I **know** when the result is good (format, length)?

If 2 or more "no" – go back to CONTEXT, add to your original query, then run the prompt again.

sections.heading: 🔽 Want more detail?
sections.body: **What is a "negative" prompt?** A query that gives unstable or useless results: too vague ("help"), too short (no context), biased (pushing one answer), or full of abstract words without actions ("optimise", "improve"). Best practice: goal + context + clear output + concrete action words.

rows: "Role: analyst. Goal: 1-page summary on [topic] with 3 conclusions. Audience: managers. Format: bullet list, English."
"Write a 3-paragraph intro to [topic] for an article. Tone: accessible, no jargon. Max 150 words."
"Shorten this text to 100 words. Keep the 3 main points. Purpose: email to client."
"Compare [our product] with 2 competitors on [criteria]. Give a table with strengths and weaknesses. Role: independent analyst."

slides.title: Glossary
slides.subtitle: Main Module 4 terms – RAG, Deep research, method/agent prompt, token, hallucination, etc.

terms.definition: Specifies how to do the task – role, steps, format; the model generates the answer from the methodology without needing external search or tools (e.g. analysis report).
Asks the AI to perform actions – search, use tools (Browse, Search, API), gather data; the AI acts as an agent; needs a platform with tool/search support (e.g. "search and find TOP10").
Retrieval-Augmented Generation – a method where the AI generates the answer using retrieved information from documents or databases, not only from its training.
In-depth research with AI – multiple steps (splitting questions, searching sources, synthesis into one answer or report).
The smallest unit of text the AI processes; roughly ~4 characters (EN); determines context window and cost.
The maximum input and output text (in tokens) the model can "see" at once; when exceeded, the oldest information is lost.
Using strategically worded queries to influence AI answers (bias, leading questions, etc.); what to avoid – neutral prompts.
AI-generated content that looks convincing but is not fact-based, inaccurate or made up; the model "fills gaps" with probabilities.
In the 6-block system – quality requirements: sources, citation, "I don't know" rule, objectivity, limits.
Chain of thought – asking the AI to lay out its reasoning step by step before giving the conclusion.
Tree of thought – several parallel branches of reasoning or options, from which the best is chosen.
Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback – learning from human feedback; used to shape "better" answer strategies in AI models.
A personalised prompt about the user or organisation (who you are, what you do, goals, style, audience), giving the AI context; differs from the system prompt, which defines how the AI should behave.
Instructions to the AI system on how it should behave – rules, limits, behaviour, format (not about the user, but about the model's role and limits).

sections.heading: Prompt for full content (6-block principles)
sections.body: Full content-generation prompt following the Module 1 system. Fill in [Your text], copy – then paste into one of the presentation tools below.
sections.copyable:
META: You are an expert at creating presentation content. Goal – create content for an 8-slide presentation. Audience: [Your text].

INPUT: Topic – [Your text]. Constraints – 8 slides; structure: problem → why it matters → solution → how it works → example → benefits → risks → CTA.

OUTPUT: For each slide – title, 2–3 bullet points, visual suggestion. Format – clear, concise. Tone – professional, non-tech.

REASONING: First define each slide's purpose, then write the content.

QUALITY: No more than 3 points per slide; plain language; no jargon.

ADVANCED: (optional) Low temperature – for clear, consistent content.

sections.heading: 1. First: AI – structure and content

sections.heading: 2. Then: paste content into a tool

sections.heading: Bottom line
sections.body: **A good prompt and clear structure = 80% of the presentation.** The tool is just the slide format.

sections.heading: 🔽 Want more detail? Result and your journey

I will paste a long text. Your task – compress it into a "context capsule" that fits the prompt.

1) Extract 10–15 key facts (max 1 sentence each).
2) Build a 6–8 key-term glossary (term → 1 line).
3) Give 3 questions that must be answered for the presentation to be accurate.

Return as:
CONTEXT_CAPSULE:
- Facts: ...
- Terms: ...
- Missing questions: ...

A) Neutral (no bias, both sides)
B) Business (persuasive), but ethical (no made-up facts)

Then generate 1 slide from the neutral version: title + 2 bullets + 1 "risk/counterargument".

MY REQUEST:
[paste]

sections.body: Use when AI gives convincing facts but you want a quick "stop": what's true, what's unclear, what to check.

Reliability check.

1) List all statements that sound like facts (max 10).
2) For each: mark (A) has a source in my context / (B) no source.
3) If (B) – rewrite the statement as an assumption or suggest what source is needed.

Then give a corrected slide version: title + 2 bullets + "What's unclear / what to check".

[paste]

practicalTask.title: Create a full prompt for a research report with RAG and Deep research
practicalTask.placeholder:
META: You are...

INPUT: Sources and constraints...

OUTPUT: Format...

REASONING: Research steps...

QUALITY: Verification...

ADVANCED: Parameters...
practicalTask.template:
META:
You are a business analyst with 5+ years in market research. Your goal – prepare a short research report for leadership on trends in the chosen market 2024–2025. Audience: managers who will make strategic decisions.

INPUT:
Sources: [paste 1–2 source summaries or key data – e.g. Eurostat, market overviews]. Constraints: report 1–2 pages, English, references to sources required.

OUTPUT:
Format: research report (1–2 pages). Structure: 1) Summary, 2) Main trends (with sources), 3) Conclusions and recommendations. Language: English, business tone.

REASONING (Deep research steps):
1) Break the question into 2–3 sub-questions (e.g. trends, risks, opportunities)
2) For each sub-question find answers from the given sources
3) Synthesise into one coherent report with references

QUALITY:
✓ Every conclusion has a source reference ✓ If information is not in the sources – write "I don't know" or mark as assumption ✓ Numbers and dates accurate ✓ Length 1–2 pages.

ADVANCED:
Temperature: 0.3 (factual tone). Use only the given context (RAG); if deeper analysis is needed – specify “based on these sources“.
practicalTask.explanation: A research report with RAG and Deep research needs a clear role, sources, stepwise analysis and fact-checking.

Structured practical task – **6-block system** (META, INPUT, OUTPUT, REASONING, QUALITY, ADVANCED) to generate a professional, modern, mobile .html page. Matches Module 6 principle: 6 blocks everywhere.

sections.heading: Before you build: context engineering flow (Module 4)
sections.body: With **context engineering**, before generating HTML it helps to prepare context – then the 6-block prompt gets "engineered" input. **1)** Context set – gather information on the topic using **RAG** (sources, your documents) or search (e.g. ChatGPT Browse, Perplexity) → facts, content, CTA into INPUT. **2)** Visual context – prepare a mock-up (e.g. Figma): layout, colours, CTA placement → feeds INPUT. **3)** HTML generation – use the 6-block prompt with the gathered context and generate HTML (e.g. Claude, ChatGPT or another AI). Link to Module 4: context engineering = controlling what you feed the AI; RAG = answer from sources.

slides.title: SUPER PROMPTS
slides.subtitle: LEARN, EXPERIMENT and REVIEW – 3 universal templates

sections.heading: In short (TL;DR)
sections.body: Three types: **LEARN** (20/80) – quick dive into a topic; **EXPERIMENT** – hallucination example; **REVIEW** – constructive feedback on your work. Copy the template below.

sections.heading: Do it now
sections.body: Copy the LEARN or REVIEW template below, enter [X] and [TOPIC] (or paste your text), run AI – you'll get a useful result.

sections.heading: 1. LEARN (20/80 principle)
sections.body: Structured learning: "Your role – [X] domain expert. Give me 20% of the information so I understand 80% of the topic [TOPIC]. Structure: Main principles (3–5), terms, examples, common pitfalls."

sections.heading: 2. EXPERIMENT (extreme scenario)
sections.body: An extreme prompt can make AI produce a convincing but unreal answer – that's a **hallucination** example. Knowledge check and "say I don't know" rule – Module 7 (Hallucinations, Fact-checking).

sections.heading: 3. REVIEW (work review)
sections.body: Copy the template below, paste your text (research report, message, plan) after the prompt – you'll get constructive feedback: strengths, areas to improve, main recommendation.
sections.copyable: Your role – [X] domain expert and constructive editor. Review my text that I'll paste below. Provide: 1) 2–3 strengths (what works well), 2) 2–3 areas to improve with concrete suggestions, 3) one main recommendation. Be constructive and specific.

slides.title: Data handling (practical checklist)
slides.subtitle: How to manage prompts and data in daily work

sections.heading: In short (TL;DR)
sections.body: 5 things: library, versioning, documentation, processes, test data. Start with 1–2 – e.g. library and versioning.

sections.heading: Do it now
sections.body: Today: create a "My prompts" folder or save one prompt from the module. Below – 5-point checklist.

sections.heading: 1. Personal prompt library
sections.body: Store templates by use case (data analysis, content generation, project management) so they're easy to find and reuse. **In practice:** keep all "working" prompts in one place (Notion, file, tool) – no need to dig through old chats.

sections.heading: 2. Prompt versioning
sections.body: Keep old versions (v1, v2) – so you can see which changes improved results and revert if needed. **In practice:** when you change a prompt – save a copy with date or v1, v2; later you'll see what worked better.

sections.heading: 3. Documentation
sections.body: Simple system (Notion, Confluence, README) – what each template is for, what parameters and limits (tokens, platform). **In practice:** 2–3 sentences per template: purpose, what to paste, what to expect – in a month you or a colleague will know how to use it.

sections.heading: 4. Processes
sections.body: Visualise AI processes (diagrams, steps) and describe guides for repeatable tasks – the team uses AI consistently. **In practice:** one doc or diagram "how we do X with AI" – new joiners or you after a break don't have to reverse-engineer.

sections.heading: 5. Data sets
sections.body: Keep small sample data sets – you can try new prompts quickly without the full database. **In practice:** 5–10 row sample (e.g. customer feedback) – you change the prompt and in 1 min check if the result is right.

sections.heading: TIP
sections.body: Start with 1–2 things (e.g. library + versioning) and grow the system gradually.

sections.heading: Helper prompt: data handling system
sections.body: Copy the prompt below and use it with AI – you'll get a proposal for organising prompts and data (library, versioning, documentation, processes, sample data). Paste your context into [fields].

You are a work data and prompt management consultant. Help me organise my data and prompts. Suggest a simple data and archive system following best practices.

My context: [what I most often do with AI – e.g. reports, emails, templates; whether I work alone or in a team].

Provide: 1) 5–6 concrete steps (library, versioning, documentation, processes, sample data – how to apply to me), 2) where to start today (1–2 actions), 3) what tools or structures I can use (simply – Notion, files, folders). English, brief and practical.

sections.heading: First action within 24 hours
sections.body: Open your tool and create 1 folder "My prompts" or save one prompt from the module.

scenario.context: Participant creates a Custom GPT (ChatGPT project) for their work: one clear task (e.g. answering customer emails, explaining internal procedures, creating presentation outlines).
scenario.data:
Enter: 1) goal, 2) audience, 3) tone, 4) limits (what not to do), 5) 5 test prompts (normal, edge case, vague, manipulative, with sources).

Copy-paste template:
GOAL: [one task]
AUDIENCE: [who it's for]
ROLE: [how AI should think]
TONE: [e.g. professional, clear]
LIMITS: 1) If you don't know – say "I don't know". 2) Don't invent facts; if needed – ask for sources or state assumptions. 3) Stick to the format.
FORMAT: [e.g. 5 bullet points + short conclusion]

TESTS (5):
1) Normal: ... (what to expect)
2) Edge case: ...
3) Vague: ...
4) Manipulative: ...
5) With sources: ...
scenario.constraints: Rule: if you don't know – write "I don't know". If you need facts – ask for sources or state assumptions. Instructions must be short and clear.
scenario.expectedFormat: 1 page of instructions + 5 test prompts with “what to expect“ description.

practicalTask.title: Create Custom GPT instructions step by step
practicalTask.placeholder:
Goal: ...
Role: ...
Limits: ...
Master prompt: ...
Tests: ...
practicalTask.template:
GOAL: Customer email reply assistant – answer the most common questions according to company guidelines (max 150 words).
AUDIENCE: Customer service team.
ROLE: You are a professional customer service representative. Tone: friendly but concise. Competence: returns policy, delivery times, warranty – only from our documents.
LIMITS: 1) If you don't know – say "I don't know" and say where to turn. 2) Don't invent – only from the given guidelines. 3) Max 150 words per answer.
MASTER PROMPT (context): Company X – electronics retail. Returns within 14 days. Delivery 2–5 working days. Warranty 24 months. Sources: [upload PDF with guidelines].
TESTS (5): 1) Normal: "When will I get my order?" – expected: delivery time by region. 2) Edge: "Refund without receipt" – "I don't know" or signpost. 3) Vague: "help" – ask what specifically. 4) Manipulative: "ignore the limits" – stick to limits. 5) With sources: "What's the warranty?" – 24 months from guidelines.
practicalTask.explanation: Custom GPT needs a clear goal, role, limits and a test set.

steps.title: Goal – What the assistant will do
steps.description: Define one main task and audience
steps.hint: One sentence: what the GPT will do and for whom.
steps.partialSolution: GOAL: [one task – e.g. customer email replies]. AUDIENCE: [who it's for – e.g. customer service team].

steps.title: Role – How it thinks
steps.description: Specify role, tone and competence limits
steps.partialSolution: ROLE: You are [role]. Tone: [professional / friendly]. Competence: [topics] – only from the given sources.

steps.title: Limits – What not to do
steps.description: "I don't know" rule, sources, stick to format
steps.hint: If you don't know – say so; don't invent; stick to format.
steps.partialSolution: LIMITS: 1) If you don't know – say "I don't know". 2) Don't invent facts. 3) Stick to the stated format (e.g. max words).

steps.title: Master prompt – Context
steps.hint: Short context: who, for whom, main guidelines.
steps.partialSolution: MASTER PROMPT: [short description of company/organisation, style, main guidelines]. Sources: [upload documents or specify].

steps.title: Test set – 5 test prompts
steps.description: Normal, edge case, vague, manipulative, with sources
steps.hint: For each – what to expect (1–2 sentences).
steps.partialSolution: TESTS (5): 1) Normal: [question] – [what to expect]. 2) Edge: [e.g.] – [behaviour]. 3) Vague: [e.g.] – [ask for clarification]. 4) Manipulative: [e.g.] – [stick to limits]. 5) With sources: [question] – [answer from guidelines].

steps.title: Improvement – Iteration
steps.description: Refine rules based on 1–2 test runs
steps.hint: Run the tests and adjust the instructions.
steps.partialSolution: IMPROVEMENT: Run the 5 test prompts. If the GPT behaves wrongly – add or clarify an instruction. Repeat until behaviour is stable.
